{{出典の明記|date=November 2019}} {{infobox religious building | name = {{ill|Myoju-in|ja|明壽院|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoju-in|qq=}} | native_name = Myoju-in | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = [[File:Myoju-in, Fushimi.jpg|300px]] | image_description = | address = [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]] [[:en:Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|Fushimi Ward]] Masuya-cho 614 | 位置 = | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 56 | 緯度秒 = 33.1 | 経度度 = 135 | 経度分 = 45 | 経度秒 = 37.7 | 山号 = {{ill|Kōenzan|ja|光圓山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōenzan|qq=}} | 院号 = | 宗旨 = | 宗派 = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|ja|真言宗醍醐派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|qq=}} | 寺格 = | 本尊 = {{ill|Seimen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seimen Kongō|qq=}} | 創建年 = [[:en:1638|1638]] | 開山 = | 開基 = | 中興年 = | 中興 = | 正式名 = | 別称 = {{ill|Fushimi Kōshin-dō|ja|伏見Kōshin-dō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fushimi Kōshin-dō|qq=}}
{{ill|Yamamura Bishamondō|ja|山村毘沙門堂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamura Bishamondō|qq=}} | 札所等 = | 文化財 = | 公式HP = | 公式HP名 = | 地図 = Japan Kyoto city | 地図2 = }} '''Myoju-in''' (みょうじゅいん, Myoju-in) is a temple, also known as '''Fushimi Kōshin-dō''' (ふしみ こうしんどう), associated with [[:en:Kōshin|Koushin Faith]]. Its mountain name is '''Kōenzan''' (こうえんざん). It is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|ja|真言宗醍醐派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|qq=}}, enshrining its principal image, the {{ill|Great Seimen Kongō|ja|大青面金剛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Seimen Kongō|qq=}} (dai-shōmen kongō, also known as "Koushin-san"). This principal image is venerated for its reputed efficacy in gathering faith to treat women's illnesses, other ailments, and to promote marital harmony. == History == The temple was founded in 1638. Initially, the temple complex included the large main hall and various subsidiary halls, along with an adjacent park area to the north of the precincts. In 1698, it became the object of a petition by the 113th Emperor of Higashiyama. During the mid-Meiji period (1868–1912), the main hall and other structures were demolished, and the temple grounds were reduced to their current scale. == Address == [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]] [[:en:Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|Fushimi Ward]] Masuya-cho 614 == Principal image == In the main hall, the principal image of the Great Seimen Kongō is enshrined. This hidden buddha (秘仏) is housed within a portable shrine (厨子内). It is exhibited only once every 60 years, in the year of {{ill|Koushin|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koushin|qq=}} (kanoesaru). It is believed to ward off severe illnesses—excluding those severe, contagious conditions known as "denshiki" (伝死鬼). Since such diseases were thought to be transmitted by unseen insects, it was believed that defeating the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) would expel these maladies, thus becoming syncretized with Koushin. Since ancient times, ritual practices have been performed here to expel all malignant spirits and demons and to fulfill all prayers. The "Seimen Kongō Dharma" is recited daily at the temple, and an imperial regalia display (御前立) is also present. Additionally, attendant deities enshrined include four Great Yasha, two Great Child Attendants, the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|Three Monkeys]] (see no evil, speak no evil, hear no evil), a waiting attendant (脇待), a statue of [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] from the Kamakura period, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|弘法大師|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūkai|qq=}}, and a tablet dedicated to [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]. == About Koushin == The term "Koushin" is derived from the combination of the [[:en:Heavenly Stems|Tenkan]] (the ten Heavenly Stems: kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki) and the [[:en:Earthly Branches|Jūnishi]] (the twelve Earthly Branches: ne, ushi, tora, u, tatsu, mi, uma, hi, saru, tori, inu, i). There are 60 possible combinations, which recur once every 60 days. On the night of a Koushin day, people remain awake in an event known as "Koushin-machi" (waiting for Koushin). It is believed that the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) reside therein. When people eventually fall asleep on a Koushin night, these insects are thought to crawl out of the body. The insects were believed to report the misdeeds committed by a person to the heavenly emperor, thereby determining that person's lifespan. Thus, by staying awake during Koushin-machi, people pray for protection, longevity, and the purity of body and spirit. == Amulets == === Kukkuri Saru === This amulet depicts a monkey with its arms and legs bound, rendering it immobile. The monkey symbolizes the desires that reside in the human heart. Just as a monkey may scurry about, these desires are thought to move within the heart, causing one to neglect effort, commit misdeeds, or hinder the fulfillment of one's wishes. By binding these desires, it is believed that Koushin controls them. Within the Kukkuri Saru, a talisman of the Great Seimen Kongō is housed, and through the secret technique of "kaigen" (awakening), a soul is imbued. == Annual Events == * Konnyaku Fūji Dai Kigan-kai (a rigorous konnyaku prayer ritual aimed at curing all diseases, joint pain, and lower back pain) (January 7) * Hoshi Matsuri (a ritual for good fortune and exorcism at the start of spring, featuring a great fire ritual with Shaitō Daigoma and fire-walking training) (February 11) * Spring Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Vernal Equinox) * Seirei Mukae Gane (a bell-ringing ritual during Obon) * Shōgaki Kuyō-kai (a Hungry Ghost Memorial Service) (August 16) * Autumn Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Autumnal Equinox) * Women's Guardian Prayer Meeting (a rigorous prayer meeting for women's happiness and protection) (3rd Sunday of October) * Emperor Higashiyama Memorial and Columbarium Mass (December 17) * Joya no Kane (bell ringing on New Year's Eve, December 31) * Monthly memorial services for ancestors and mizuko (water child) memorial services, as well as Koushin Goma (on the 7th day of every month) * Prayer meetings (on Koushin days) {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Kyoto Prefecture}} ]] }} {{translated page|ja|明壽院|version=101308058|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| {{draft categories| [[Category:ill|Temples in Fushimi Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市伏見区の寺}} [[sn:Myoju-in]] }} [[ja:明壽院]] [[en:明壽院]] [[sn:明壽院]] [[Category:Temples in Fushimi-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:真言宗醍醐派の寺院]]

{{出典の明記|date=November 2019}} {{infobox religious building | name = {{ill|Myoju-in|ja|明壽院|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoju-in|qq=}} | native_name = Myoju-in | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = [[File:Myoju-in, Fushimi.jpg|300px]] | image_description = | address = [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]] [[:en:Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|Fushimi Ward]] Masuya-cho 614 | 位置 = | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 56 | 緯度秒 = 33.1 | 経度度 = 135 | 経度分 = 45 | 経度秒 = 37.7 | 山号 = {{ill|Kōenzan|ja|光圓山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōenzan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | 院号 = | 宗旨 = | 宗派 = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|ja|真言宗醍醐派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|qq=}} | 寺格 = | 本尊 = {{ill|Seimen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seimen Kongō|qq=}} | 創建年 = [[:en:1638|1638]] | 開山 = | 開基 = | 中興年 = | 中興 = | 正式名 = | 別称 = {{ill|Fushimi Kōshin-dō|ja|伏見Kōshin-dō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fushimi Kōshin-dō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{ill|Yamamura Bishamondō|ja|山村毘沙門堂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamura Bishamondō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 円満寺 (奈良市)}} | 札所等 = | 文化財 = | 公式HP = | 公式HP名 = | 地図 = Japan Kyoto city | 地図2 = }} '''Myoju-in''' (みょうじゅいん, Myoju-in) is a temple, also known as '''Fushimi Kōshin-dō''' (ふしみ こうしんどう), associated with [[:en:Kōshin|Koushin Faith]]. Its mountain name is '''Kōenzan''' (こうえんざん). It is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|ja|真言宗醍醐派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|qq=}}, enshrining its principal image, the {{ill|Great Seimen Kongō|ja|大青面金剛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Seimen Kongō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (dai-shōmen kongō, also known as "Koushin-san"). This principal image is venerated for its reputed efficacy in gathering faith to treat women's illnesses, other ailments, and to promote marital harmony. == History == The temple was founded in 1638. Initially, the temple complex included the large main hall and various subsidiary halls, along with an adjacent park area to the north of the precincts. In 1698, it became the object of a petition by the 113th Emperor of Higashiyama. During the mid-Meiji period (1868–1912), the main hall and other structures were demolished, and the temple grounds were reduced to their current scale. == Address == [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]] [[:en:Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|Fushimi Ward]] Masuya-cho 614 == Principal image == In the main hall, the principal image of the Great Seimen Kongō is enshrined. This hidden buddha (秘仏) is housed within a portable shrine (厨子内). It is exhibited only once every 60 years, in the year of {{ill|Koushin|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koushin|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (kanoesaru). It is believed to ward off severe illnesses—excluding those severe, contagious conditions known as "denshiki" (伝死鬼). Since such diseases were thought to be transmitted by unseen insects, it was believed that defeating the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) would expel these maladies, thus becoming syncretized with Koushin. Since ancient times, ritual practices have been performed here to expel all malignant spirits and demons and to fulfill all prayers. The "Seimen Kongō Dharma" is recited daily at the temple, and an imperial regalia display (御前立) is also present. Additionally, attendant deities enshrined include four Great Yasha, two Great Child Attendants, the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|Three Monkeys]] (see no evil, speak no evil, hear no evil), a waiting attendant (脇待), a statue of [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] from the Kamakura period, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|弘法大師|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūkai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 空海}}, and a tablet dedicated to [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]. == About Koushin == The term "Koushin" is derived from the combination of the [[:en:Heavenly Stems|Tenkan]] (the ten Heavenly Stems: kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki) and the [[:en:Earthly Branches|Jūnishi]] (the twelve Earthly Branches: ne, ushi, tora, u, tatsu, mi, uma, hi, saru, tori, inu, i). There are 60 possible combinations, which recur once every 60 days. On the night of a Koushin day, people remain awake in an event known as "Koushin-machi" (waiting for Koushin). It is believed that the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) reside therein. When people eventually fall asleep on a Koushin night, these insects are thought to crawl out of the body. The insects were believed to report the misdeeds committed by a person to the heavenly emperor, thereby determining that person's lifespan. Thus, by staying awake during Koushin-machi, people pray for protection, longevity, and the purity of body and spirit. == Amulets == === Kukkuri Saru === This amulet depicts a monkey with its arms and legs bound, rendering it immobile. The monkey symbolizes the desires that reside in the human heart. Just as a monkey may scurry about, these desires are thought to move within the heart, causing one to neglect effort, commit misdeeds, or hinder the fulfillment of one's wishes. By binding these desires, it is believed that Koushin controls them. Within the Kukkuri Saru, a talisman of the Great Seimen Kongō is housed, and through the secret technique of "kaigen" (awakening), a soul is imbued. == Annual Events == * Konnyaku Fūji Dai Kigan-kai (a rigorous konnyaku prayer ritual aimed at curing all diseases, joint pain, and lower back pain) (January 7) * Hoshi Matsuri (a ritual for good fortune and exorcism at the start of spring, featuring a great fire ritual with Shaitō Daigoma and fire-walking training) (February 11) * Spring Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Vernal Equinox) * Seirei Mukae Gane (a bell-ringing ritual during Obon) * Shōgaki Kuyō-kai (a Hungry Ghost Memorial Service) (August 16) * Autumn Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Autumnal Equinox) * Women's Guardian Prayer Meeting (a rigorous prayer meeting for women's happiness and protection) (3rd Sunday of October) * Emperor Higashiyama Memorial and Columbarium Mass (December 17) * Joya no Kane (bell ringing on New Year's Eve, December 31) * Monthly memorial services for ancestors and mizuko (water child) memorial services, as well as Koushin Goma (on the 7th day of every month) * Prayer meetings (on Koushin days) {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Kyoto Prefecture}} ]] }} {{translated page|ja|明壽院|version=101308058|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| {{draft categories| [[Category:ill|Temples in Fushimi Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市伏見区の寺}} [[sn:Myoju-in]] }} [[ja:明壽院]] [[sn:明壽院]] [[Category:Temples in Fushimi-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:Temples of the Shingon Daigo branch]]

{{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Myoju-in, Fushimi.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon-shū Daigo-ha|qid=Q10437874}}|prefecture={{ill|Fushimi-ku|qid=Q1197771}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.94252777777778|N|135.76047222222223|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q85880838]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{出典の明記|date=November 2019}} {{infobox religious building | name = {{ill|Myoju-in|ja|明壽院|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoju-in|qq=}} | native_name = Myoju-in | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = [[File:Myoju-in, Fushimi.jpg|300px]] | image_description = | address = [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]] [[:en:Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|Fushimi Ward]] Masuya-cho 614 | 位置 = | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 56 | 緯度秒 = 33.1 | 経度度 = 135 | 経度分 = 45 | 経度秒 = 37.7 | 山号 = {{ill|Kōenzan|ja|光圓山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōenzan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | 院号 = | 宗旨 = | 宗派 = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|ja|真言宗醍醐派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|qq=}} | 寺格 = | 本尊 = {{ill|Seimen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seimen Kongō|qq=}} | 創建年 = [[:en:1638|1638]] | 開山 = | 開基 = | 中興年 = | 中興 = | 正式名 = | 別称 = {{ill|Fushimi Kōshin-dō|ja|伏見Kōshin-dō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fushimi Kōshin-dō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{ill|Yamamura Bishamondō|ja|山村毘沙門堂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamura Bishamondō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 円満寺 (奈良市)}} | 札所等 = | 文化財 = | 公式HP = | 公式HP名 = | 地図 = Japan Kyoto city | 地図2 = }} '''Myoju-in''' (みょうじゅいん, Myoju-in) is a temple, also known as '''Fushimi Kōshin-dō''' (ふしみ こうしんどう), associated with [[:en:Kōshin|Koushin Faith]]. Its mountain name is '''Kōenzan''' (こうえんざん). It is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|ja|真言宗醍醐派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|qq=}}, enshrining its principal image, the {{ill|Great Seimen Kongō|ja|大青面金剛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Seimen Kongō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (dai-shōmen kongō, also known as "Koushin-san"). This principal image is venerated for its reputed efficacy in gathering faith to treat women's illnesses, other ailments, and to promote marital harmony. == History == The temple was founded in 1638. Initially, the temple complex included the large main hall and various subsidiary halls, along with an adjacent park area to the north of the precincts. In 1698, it became the object of a petition by the 113th Emperor of Higashiyama. During the mid-Meiji period (1868–1912), the main hall and other structures were demolished, and the temple grounds were reduced to their current scale. == Address == [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]] [[:en:Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|Fushimi Ward]] Masuya-cho 614 == Principal image == In the main hall, the principal image of the Great Seimen Kongō is enshrined. This hidden buddha (秘仏) is housed within a portable shrine (厨子内). It is exhibited only once every 60 years, in the year of {{ill|Koushin|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koushin|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (kanoesaru). It is believed to ward off severe illnesses—excluding those severe, contagious conditions known as "denshiki" (伝死鬼). Since such diseases were thought to be transmitted by unseen insects, it was believed that defeating the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) would expel these maladies, thus becoming syncretized with Koushin. Since ancient times, ritual practices have been performed here to expel all malignant spirits and demons and to fulfill all prayers. The "Seimen Kongō Dharma" is recited daily at the temple, and an imperial regalia display (御前立) is also present. Additionally, attendant deities enshrined include four Great Yasha, two Great Child Attendants, the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|Three Monkeys]] (see no evil, speak no evil, hear no evil), a waiting attendant (脇待), a statue of [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] from the Kamakura period, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|弘法大師|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūkai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 空海}}, and a tablet dedicated to [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]. == About Koushin == The term "Koushin" is derived from the combination of the [[:en:Heavenly Stems|Tenkan]] (the ten Heavenly Stems: kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki) and the [[:en:Earthly Branches|Jūnishi]] (the twelve Earthly Branches: ne, ushi, tora, u, tatsu, mi, uma, hi, saru, tori, inu, i). There are 60 possible combinations, which recur once every 60 days. On the night of a Koushin day, people remain awake in an event known as "Koushin-machi" (waiting for Koushin). It is believed that the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) reside therein. When people eventually fall asleep on a Koushin night, these insects are thought to crawl out of the body. The insects were believed to report the misdeeds committed by a person to the heavenly emperor, thereby determining that person's lifespan. Thus, by staying awake during Koushin-machi, people pray for protection, longevity, and the purity of body and spirit. == Amulets == === Kukkuri Saru === This amulet depicts a monkey with its arms and legs bound, rendering it immobile. The monkey symbolizes the desires that reside in the human heart. Just as a monkey may scurry about, these desires are thought to move within the heart, causing one to neglect effort, commit misdeeds, or hinder the fulfillment of one's wishes. By binding these desires, it is believed that Koushin controls them. Within the Kukkuri Saru, a talisman of the Great Seimen Kongō is housed, and through the secret technique of "kaigen" (awakening), a soul is imbued. == Annual Events == * Konnyaku Fūji Dai Kigan-kai (a rigorous konnyaku prayer ritual aimed at curing all diseases, joint pain, and lower back pain) (January 7) * Hoshi Matsuri (a ritual for good fortune and exorcism at the start of spring, featuring a great fire ritual with Shaitō Daigoma and fire-walking training) (February 11) * Spring Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Vernal Equinox) * Seirei Mukae Gane (a bell-ringing ritual during Obon) * Shōgaki Kuyō-kai (a Hungry Ghost Memorial Service) (August 16) * Autumn Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Autumnal Equinox) * Women's Guardian Prayer Meeting (a rigorous prayer meeting for women's happiness and protection) (3rd Sunday of October) * Emperor Higashiyama Memorial and Columbarium Mass (December 17) * Joya no Kane (bell ringing on New Year's Eve, December 31) * Monthly memorial services for ancestors and mizuko (water child) memorial services, as well as Koushin Goma (on the 7th day of every month) * Prayer meetings (on Koushin days) {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Kyoto Prefecture}} }} {{translated page|ja|明壽院|version=101308058|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| {{draft categories| [[Category:ill|Temples in Fushimi Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市伏見区の寺}} [[sn:Myoju-in]] }} [[ja:明壽院]] [[sn:明壽院]] [[Category:Temples in Fushimi-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:Temples of the Shingon Daigo branch]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]]

By binding these desires, it is believed that Koushin controls them. Within the Kukkuri Saru, a talisman of the Great Seimen Kongō is housed, and through the secret technique of "kaigen" (awakening), a soul is imbued. == Annual Events == * Konnyaku Fūji Dai Kigan-kai (a rigorous konnyaku prayer ritual aimed at curing all diseases, joint pain, and lower back pain) (January 7) * Hoshi Matsuri (a ritual for good fortune and exorcism at the start of spring, featuring a great fire ritual with Shaitō Daigoma and fire-walking training) (February 11) * Spring Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Vernal Equinox) * Seirei Mukae Gane (a bell-ringing ritual during Obon) * Shōgaki Kuyō-kai (a Hungry Ghost Memorial Service) (August 16) * Autumn Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Autumnal Equinox) * Women's Guardian Prayer Meeting (a rigorous prayer meeting for women's happiness and protection) (3rd Sunday of October) * Emperor Higashiyama Memorial and Columbarium Mass (December 17) * Joya no Kane (bell ringing on New Year's Eve, December 31) * Monthly memorial services for ancestors and mizuko (water child) memorial services, as well as Koushin Goma (on the 7th day of every month) * Prayer meetings (on Koushin days) {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Kyoto Prefecture}} }} {{translated page|ja|明壽院|version=101308058|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| {{draft categories| [[Category:ill|Temples in Fushimi Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市伏見区の寺}} [[sn:Myoju-in]] }} [[ja:明壽院]] [[sn:明壽院]] [[Category:Temples in Fushimi-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:Temples of the Shingon Daigo branch]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] {{wikidata link|Q85880838}}

{{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Myoju-in, Fushimi.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon-shū Daigo-ha|qid=Q10437874}}|prefecture={{ill|Fushimi-ku|qid=Q1197771}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.94252777777778|N|135.76047222222223|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q85880838]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{出典の明記|date=November 2019}} {{infobox religious building | name = {{ill|Myoju-in|ja|明壽院|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoju-in|qq=}} | native_name = Myoju-in | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = [[File:Myoju-in, Fushimi.jpg|300px]] | image_description = | address = [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]] [[:en:Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|Fushimi Ward]] Masuya-cho 614 | 位置 = | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 56 | 緯度秒 = 33.1 | 経度度 = 135 | 経度分 = 45 | 経度秒 = 37.7 | 山号 = {{ill|Kōenzan|ja|光圓山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōenzan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | 院号 = | 宗旨 = | 宗派 = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|ja|真言宗醍醐派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|qq=|1=Shingon-shū Daigo-ha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10437874}} | 寺格 = | 本尊 = {{ill|Seimen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seimen Kongō|qq=|1=Shōmen Kongō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11662826}} | 創建年 = [[:en:1638|1638]] | 開山 = | 開基 = | 中興年 = | 中興 = | 正式名 = | 別称 = {{ill|Fushimi Kōshin-dō|ja|伏見Kōshin-dō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fushimi Kōshin-dō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{ill|Yamamura Bishamondō|ja|山村毘沙門堂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamura Bishamondō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 円満寺 (奈良市)|1=Enman-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394813}} | 札所等 = | 文化財 = | 公式HP = | 公式HP名 = | 地図 = Japan Kyoto city | 地図2 = }} '''Myoju-in''' (みょうじゅいん, Myoju-in) is a temple, also known as '''Fushimi Kōshin-dō''' (ふしみ こうしんどう), associated with [[:en:Kōshin|Koushin Faith]]. Its mountain name is '''Kōenzan''' (こうえんざん). It is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|ja|真言宗醍醐派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|qq=|1=Shingon-shū Daigo-ha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10437874}}, enshrining its principal image, the {{ill|Great Seimen Kongō|ja|大青面金剛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Seimen Kongō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (dai-shōmen kongō, also known as "Koushin-san"). This principal image is venerated for its reputed efficacy in gathering faith to treat women's illnesses, other ailments, and to promote marital harmony. == History == The temple was founded in 1638. Initially, the temple complex included the large main hall and various subsidiary halls, along with an adjacent park area to the north of the precincts. In 1698, it became the object of a petition by the 113th Emperor of Higashiyama. During the mid-Meiji period (1868–1912), the main hall and other structures were demolished, and the temple grounds were reduced to their current scale. == Address == [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]] [[:en:Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|Fushimi Ward]] Masuya-cho 614 == Principal image == In the main hall, the principal image of the Great Seimen Kongō is enshrined. This hidden buddha (秘仏) is housed within a portable shrine (厨子内). It is exhibited only once every 60 years, in the year of {{ill|Koushin|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koushin|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (kanoesaru). It is believed to ward off severe illnesses—excluding those severe, contagious conditions known as "denshiki" (伝死鬼). Since such diseases were thought to be transmitted by unseen insects, it was believed that defeating the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) would expel these maladies, thus becoming syncretized with Koushin. Since ancient times, ritual practices have been performed here to expel all malignant spirits and demons and to fulfill all prayers. The "Seimen Kongō Dharma" is recited daily at the temple, and an imperial regalia display (御前立) is also present. Additionally, attendant deities enshrined include four Great Yasha, two Great Child Attendants, the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|Three Monkeys]] (see no evil, speak no evil, hear no evil), a waiting attendant (脇待), a statue of [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] from the Kamakura period, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|弘法大師|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūkai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 空海|1=Kūkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q222227}}, and a tablet dedicated to [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]. == About Koushin == The term "Koushin" is derived from the combination of the [[:en:Heavenly Stems|Tenkan]] (the ten Heavenly Stems: kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki) and the [[:en:Earthly Branches|Jūnishi]] (the twelve Earthly Branches: ne, ushi, tora, u, tatsu, mi, uma, hi, saru, tori, inu, i). There are 60 possible combinations, which recur once every 60 days. On the night of a Koushin day, people remain awake in an event known as "Koushin-machi" (waiting for Koushin). It is believed that the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) reside therein. When people eventually fall asleep on a Koushin night, these insects are thought to crawl out of the body. The insects were believed to report the misdeeds committed by a person to the heavenly emperor, thereby determining that person's lifespan. Thus, by staying awake during Koushin-machi, people pray for protection, longevity, and the purity of body and spirit. == Amulets == === Kukkuri Saru === This amulet depicts a monkey with its arms and legs bound, rendering it immobile. The monkey symbolizes the desires that reside in the human heart. Just as a monkey may scurry about, these desires are thought to move within the heart, causing one to neglect effort, commit misdeeds, or hinder the fulfillment of one's wishes. By binding these desires, it is believed that Koushin controls them. Within the Kukkuri Saru, a talisman of the Great Seimen Kongō is housed, and through the secret technique of "kaigen" (awakening), a soul is imbued. == Annual Events == * Konnyaku Fūji Dai Kigan-kai (a rigorous konnyaku prayer ritual aimed at curing all diseases, joint pain, and lower back pain) (January 7) * Hoshi Matsuri (a ritual for good fortune and exorcism at the start of spring, featuring a great fire ritual with Shaitō Daigoma and fire-walking training) (February 11) * Spring Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Vernal Equinox) * Seirei Mukae Gane (a bell-ringing ritual during Obon) * Shōgaki Kuyō-kai (a Hungry Ghost Memorial Service) (August 16) * Autumn Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Autumnal Equinox) * Women's Guardian Prayer Meeting (a rigorous prayer meeting for women's happiness and protection) (3rd Sunday of October) * Emperor Higashiyama Memorial and Columbarium Mass (December 17) * Joya no Kane (bell ringing on New Year's Eve, December 31) * Monthly memorial services for ancestors and mizuko (water child) memorial services, as well as Koushin Goma (on the 7th day of every month) * Prayer meetings (on Koushin days) {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Kyoto Prefecture}} }} {{translated page|ja|明壽院|version=101308058|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| {{draft categories| [[Category:ill|Temples in Fushimi Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市伏見区の寺}} }} [[ja:明壽院]] [[Category:Temples in Fushimi-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:Temples of the Shingon Daigo branch]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] {{wikidata link|Q85880838}}

There are 60 possible combinations, which recur once every 60 days. On the night of a Koushin day, people remain awake in an event known as "Koushin-machi" (waiting for Koushin). It is believed that the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) reside therein. When people eventually fall asleep on a Koushin night, these insects are thought to crawl out of the body. The insects were believed to report the misdeeds committed by a person to the heavenly emperor, thereby determining that person's lifespan. Thus, by staying awake during Koushin-machi, people pray for protection, longevity, and the purity of body and spirit. == Amulets == === Kukkuri Saru === This amulet depicts a monkey with its arms and legs bound, rendering it immobile. The monkey symbolizes the desires that reside in the human heart. Just as a monkey may scurry about, these desires are thought to move within the heart, causing one to neglect effort, commit misdeeds, or hinder the fulfillment of one's wishes. By binding these desires, it is believed that Koushin controls them. Within the Kukkuri Saru, a talisman of the Great Seimen Kongō is housed, and through the secret technique of "kaigen" (awakening), a soul is imbued. == Annual Events == * Konnyaku Fūji Dai Kigan-kai (a rigorous konnyaku prayer ritual aimed at curing all diseases, joint pain, and lower back pain) (January 7) * Hoshi Matsuri (a ritual for good fortune and exorcism at the start of spring, featuring a great fire ritual with Shaitō Daigoma and fire-walking training) (February 11) * Spring Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Vernal Equinox) * Seirei Mukae Gane (a bell-ringing ritual during Obon) * Shōgaki Kuyō-kai (a Hungry Ghost Memorial Service) (August 16) * Autumn Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Autumnal Equinox) * Women's Guardian Prayer Meeting (a rigorous prayer meeting for women's happiness and protection) (3rd Sunday of October) * Emperor Higashiyama Memorial and Columbarium Mass (December 17) * Joya no Kane (bell ringing on New Year's Eve, December 31) * Monthly memorial services for ancestors and mizuko (water child) memorial services, as well as Koushin Goma (on the 7th day of every month) * Prayer meetings (on Koushin days) {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Kyoto Prefecture}} }} {{translated page|ja|明壽院|version=101308058|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| {{draft categories| [[Category:ill|Temples in Fushimi Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市伏見区の寺}} }} [[ja:明壽院]] [[Category:Temples in Fushimi-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:Temples of the Shingon Daigo branch]] [[Category:qq]] {{wikidata link|Q85880838}}

{{ill|Yamamura Bishamondō|ja|山村毘沙門堂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamura Bishamondō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 円満寺 (奈良市)|1=Enman-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394813}} | 札所等 = | 文化財 = | 公式HP = | 公式HP名 = | 地図 = Japan Kyoto city | 地図2 = }} '''Myoju-in''' (みょうじゅいん, Myoju-in) is a temple, also known as '''Fushimi Kōshin-dō''' (ふしみ こうしんどう), associated with [[:en:Kōshin|Koushin Faith]]. Its mountain name is '''Kōenzan''' (こうえんざん). It is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|ja|真言宗醍醐派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|qq=|1=Shingon-shū Daigo-ha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10437874}}, enshrining its principal image, the {{ill|Great Seimen Kongō|ja|大青面金剛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Seimen Kongō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (dai-shōmen kongō, also known as "Koushin-san"). This principal image is venerated for its reputed efficacy in gathering faith to treat women's illnesses, other ailments, and to promote marital harmony. == History == The temple was founded in 1638. Initially, the temple complex included the large main hall and various subsidiary halls, along with an adjacent park area to the north of the precincts. In 1698, it became the object of a petition by the 113th Emperor of Higashiyama. During the mid-Meiji period (1868–1912), the main hall and other structures were demolished, and the temple grounds were reduced to their current scale. == Address == [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]] [[:en:Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|Fushimi Ward]] Masuya-cho 614 == Principal image == In the main hall, the principal image of the Great Seimen Kongō is enshrined. This hidden buddha (秘仏) is housed within a portable shrine (厨子内). It is exhibited only once every 60 years, in the year of {{ill|Koushin|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koushin|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (kanoesaru). It is believed to ward off severe illnesses—excluding those severe, contagious conditions known as "denshiki" (伝死鬼). Since such diseases were thought to be transmitted by unseen insects, it was believed that defeating the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) would expel these maladies, thus becoming syncretized with Koushin. Since ancient times, ritual practices have been performed here to expel all malignant spirits and demons and to fulfill all prayers. The "Seimen Kongō Dharma" is recited daily at the temple, and an imperial regalia display (御前立) is also present. Additionally, attendant deities enshrined include four Great Yasha, two Great Child Attendants, the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|Three Monkeys]] (see no evil, speak no evil, hear no evil), a waiting attendant (脇待), a statue of [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] from the Kamakura period, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|弘法大師|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūkai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 空海|1=Kūkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q222227}}, and a tablet dedicated to [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]. == About Koushin == The term "Koushin" is derived from the combination of the [[:en:Heavenly Stems|Tenkan]] (the ten Heavenly Stems: kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki) and the [[:en:Earthly Branches|Jūnishi]] (the twelve Earthly Branches: ne, ushi, tora, u, tatsu, mi, uma, hi, saru, tori, inu, i). There are 60 possible combinations, which recur once every 60 days. On the night of a Koushin day, people remain awake in an event known as "Koushin-machi" (waiting for Koushin). It is believed that the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) reside therein. When people eventually fall asleep on a Koushin night, these insects are thought to crawl out of the body. The insects were believed to report the misdeeds committed by a person to the heavenly emperor, thereby determining that person's lifespan. Thus, by staying awake during Koushin-machi, people pray for protection, longevity, and the purity of body and spirit. == Amulets == === Kukkuri Saru === This amulet depicts a monkey with its arms and legs bound, rendering it immobile. The monkey symbolizes the desires that reside in the human heart. Just as a monkey may scurry about, these desires are thought to move within the heart, causing one to neglect effort, commit misdeeds, or hinder the fulfillment of one's wishes. By binding these desires, it is believed that Koushin controls them. Within the Kukkuri Saru, a talisman of the Great Seimen Kongō is housed, and through the secret technique of "kaigen" (awakening), a soul is imbued. == Annual Events == * Konnyaku Fūji Dai Kigan-kai (a rigorous konnyaku prayer ritual aimed at curing all diseases, joint pain, and lower back pain) (January 7) * Hoshi Matsuri (a ritual for good fortune and exorcism at the start of spring, featuring a great fire ritual with Shaitō Daigoma and fire-walking training) (February 11) * Spring Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Vernal Equinox) * Seirei Mukae Gane (a bell-ringing ritual during Obon) * Shōgaki Kuyō-kai (a Hungry Ghost Memorial Service) (August 16) * Autumn Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Autumnal Equinox) * Women's Guardian Prayer Meeting (a rigorous prayer meeting for women's happiness and protection) (3rd Sunday of October) * Emperor Higashiyama Memorial and Columbarium Mass (December 17) * Joya no Kane (bell ringing on New Year's Eve, December 31) * Monthly memorial services for ancestors and mizuko (water child) memorial services, as well as Koushin Goma (on the 7th day of every month) * Prayer meetings (on Koushin days) {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Kyoto Prefecture}} }} {{translated page|ja|明壽院|version=101308058|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| {{draft categories| [[Category:ill|Temples in Fushimi Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市伏見区の寺}} }} [[ja:明壽院]] [[Category:Temples in Fushimi-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:Temples of the Shingon Daigo branch]] {{wikidata link|Q85880838}}

It is believed to ward off severe illnesses—excluding those severe, contagious conditions known as "denshiki" (伝死鬼). Since such diseases were thought to be transmitted by unseen insects, it was believed that defeating the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) would expel these maladies, thus becoming syncretized with Koushin. Since ancient times, ritual practices have been performed here to expel all malignant spirits and demons and to fulfill all prayers. The "Seimen Kongō Dharma" is recited daily at the temple, and an imperial regalia display (御前立) is also present. Additionally, attendant deities enshrined include four Great Yasha, two Great Child Attendants, the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|Three Monkeys]] (see no evil, speak no evil, hear no evil), a waiting attendant (脇待), a statue of [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] from the Kamakura period, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|弘法大師|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūkai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 空海|1=Kūkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q222227}}, and a tablet dedicated to [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]. == About Koushin == The term "Koushin" is derived from the combination of the [[:en:Heavenly Stems|Tenkan]] (the ten Heavenly Stems: kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki) and the [[:en:Earthly Branches|Jūnishi]] (the twelve Earthly Branches: ne, ushi, tora, u, tatsu, mi, uma, hi, saru, tori, inu, i). There are 60 possible combinations, which recur once every 60 days. On the night of a Koushin day, people remain awake in an event known as "Koushin-machi" (waiting for Koushin). It is believed that the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) reside therein. When people eventually fall asleep on a Koushin night, these insects are thought to crawl out of the body. The insects were believed to report the misdeeds committed by a person to the heavenly emperor, thereby determining that person's lifespan. Thus, by staying awake during Koushin-machi, people pray for protection, longevity, and the purity of body and spirit. == Amulets == === Kukkuri Saru === This amulet depicts a monkey with its arms and legs bound, rendering it immobile. The monkey symbolizes the desires that reside in the human heart. Just as a monkey may scurry about, these desires are thought to move within the heart, causing one to neglect effort, commit misdeeds, or hinder the fulfillment of one's wishes. By binding these desires, it is believed that Koushin controls them. Within the Kukkuri Saru, a talisman of the Great Seimen Kongō is housed, and through the secret technique of "kaigen" (awakening), a soul is imbued. == Annual Events == * Konnyaku Fūji Dai Kigan-kai (a rigorous konnyaku prayer ritual aimed at curing all diseases, joint pain, and lower back pain) (January 7) * Hoshi Matsuri (a ritual for good fortune and exorcism at the start of spring, featuring a great fire ritual with Shaitō Daigoma and fire-walking training) (February 11) * Spring Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Vernal Equinox) * Seirei Mukae Gane (a bell-ringing ritual during Obon) * Shōgaki Kuyō-kai (a Hungry Ghost Memorial Service) (August 16) * Autumn Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Autumnal Equinox) * Women's Guardian Prayer Meeting (a rigorous prayer meeting for women's happiness and protection) (3rd Sunday of October) * Emperor Higashiyama Memorial and Columbarium Mass (December 17) * Joya no Kane (bell ringing on New Year's Eve, December 31) * Monthly memorial services for ancestors and mizuko (water child) memorial services, as well as Koushin Goma (on the 7th day of every month) * Prayer meetings (on Koushin days) {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Kyoto Prefecture}} }} {{translated page|ja|明壽院|version=101308058|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| {{draft categories| [[Category:ill|Temples in Fushimi Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市伏見区の寺}} }} [[ja:明壽院]] [[Category:Temples in Fushimi-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:Temples of the Shingon Daigo branch]] {{wikidata link|Q85880838}}

{{ill|Yamamura Bishamondō|ja|山村毘沙門堂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamura Bishamondō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 円満寺 (奈良市)|1=Enman-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394813}} | 札所等 = | 文化財 = | 公式HP = | 公式HP名 = | 地図 = Japan Kyoto city | 地図2 = }} '''Myoju-in''' (みょうじゅいん, Myoju-in) is a temple, also known as '''Fushimi Kōshin-dō''' (ふしみ こうしんどう), associated with [[:en:Kōshin|Koushin Faith]]. Its mountain name is '''Kōenzan''' (こうえんざん). It is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|ja|真言宗醍醐派|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|qq=|1=Shingon-shū Daigo-ha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10437874}}, enshrining its principal image, the {{ill|Great Seimen Kongō|ja|大青面金剛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Seimen Kongō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (dai-shōmen kongō, also known as "Koushin-san"). This principal image is venerated for its reputed efficacy in gathering faith to treat women's illnesses, other ailments, and to promote marital harmony. == History == The temple was founded in 1638. Initially, the temple complex included the large main hall and various subsidiary halls, along with an adjacent park area to the north of the precincts. In 1698, it became the object of a petition by the 113th Emperor of Higashiyama. During the mid-Meiji period (1868–1912), the main hall and other structures were demolished, and the temple grounds were reduced to their current scale. == Address == [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]] [[:en:Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|Fushimi Ward]] Masuya-cho 614 == Principal image == In the main hall, the principal image of the Great Seimen Kongō is enshrined. During the mid-Meiji period (1868–1912), the main hall and other structures were demolished, and the temple grounds were reduced to their current scale. == Address == [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]] [[:en:Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|Fushimi Ward]] Masuya-cho 614 == Principal image == In the main hall, the principal image of the Great Seimen Kongō is enshrined. This hidden buddha (秘仏) is housed within a portable shrine (厨子内). It is exhibited only once every 60 years, in the year of {{ill|Koushin|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koushin|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (kanoesaru). It is believed to ward off severe illnesses—excluding those severe, contagious conditions known as "denshiki" (伝死鬼). Since such diseases were thought to be transmitted by unseen insects, it was believed that defeating the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) would expel these maladies, thus becoming syncretized with Koushin. Since ancient times, ritual practices have been performed here to expel all malignant spirits and demons and to fulfill all prayers. The "Seimen Kongō Dharma" is recited daily at the temple, and an imperial regalia display (御前立) is also present. Additionally, attendant deities enshrined include four Great Yasha, two Great Child Attendants, the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|Three Monkeys]] (see no evil, speak no evil, hear no evil), a waiting attendant (脇待), a statue of [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] from the Kamakura period, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|弘法大師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūkai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 空海|1=Kūkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q222227}}, and a tablet dedicated to [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]. == About Koushin == The term "Koushin" is derived from the combination of the [[:en:Heavenly Stems|Tenkan]] (the ten Heavenly Stems: kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki) and the [[:en:Earthly Branches|Jūnishi]] (the twelve Earthly Branches: ne, ushi, tora, u, tatsu, mi, uma, hi, saru, tori, inu, i). There are 60 possible combinations, which recur once every 60 days. On the night of a Koushin day, people remain awake in an event known as "Koushin-machi" (waiting for Koushin). It is believed that the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) reside therein. When people eventually fall asleep on a Koushin night, these insects are thought to crawl out of the body. The insects were believed to report the misdeeds committed by a person to the heavenly emperor, thereby determining that person's lifespan. Thus, by staying awake during Koushin-machi, people pray for protection, longevity, and the purity of body and spirit. == Amulets == === Kukkuri Saru === This amulet depicts a monkey with its arms and legs bound, rendering it immobile. The monkey symbolizes the desires that reside in the human heart. Just as a monkey may scurry about, these desires are thought to move within the heart, causing one to neglect effort, commit misdeeds, or hinder the fulfillment of one's wishes. By binding these desires, it is believed that Koushin controls them. Within the Kukkuri Saru, a talisman of the Great Seimen Kongō is housed, and through the secret technique of "kaigen" (awakening), a soul is imbued. == Annual Events == * Konnyaku Fūji Dai Kigan-kai (a rigorous konnyaku prayer ritual aimed at curing all diseases, joint pain, and lower back pain) (January 7) * Hoshi Matsuri (a ritual for good fortune and exorcism at the start of spring, featuring a great fire ritual with Shaitō Daigoma and fire-walking training) (February 11) * Spring Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Vernal Equinox) * Seirei Mukae Gane (a bell-ringing ritual during Obon) * Shōgaki Kuyō-kai (a Hungry Ghost Memorial Service) (August 16) * Autumn Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Autumnal Equinox) * Women's Guardian Prayer Meeting (a rigorous prayer meeting for women's happiness and protection) (3rd Sunday of October) * Emperor Higashiyama Memorial and Columbarium Mass (December 17) * Joya no Kane (bell ringing on New Year's Eve, December 31) * Monthly memorial services for ancestors and mizuko (water child) memorial services, as well as Koushin Goma (on the 7th day of every month) * Prayer meetings (on Koushin days) {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Kyoto Prefecture}} }} {{translated page|ja|明壽院|version=101308058|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| {{draft categories| [[Category:ill|Temples in Fushimi Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市伏見区の寺}} }} [[ja:明壽院]] [[Category:Temples in Fushimi-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:Temples of the Shingon Daigo branch]] {{wikidata link|Q85880838}}

{{ill|Yamamura Bishamondō|ja|山村毘沙門堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamura Bishamondō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 円満寺 (奈良市)|1=Enman-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394813}} | 札所等 = | 文化財 = | 公式HP = | 公式HP名 = | 地図 = Japan Kyoto city | 地図2 = }} '''Myoju-in''' (みょうじゅいん, Myoju-in) is a temple, also known as '''Fushimi Kōshin-dō''' (ふしみ こうしんどう), associated with [[:en:Kōshin|Koushin Faith]]. Its mountain name is '''Kōenzan''' (こうえんざん). It is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|ja|真言宗醍醐派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|1=Shingon-shū Daigo-ha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10437874}}, enshrining its principal image, the {{ill|Great Seimen Kongō|ja|大青面金剛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Seimen Kongō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (dai-shōmen kongō, also known as "Koushin-san"). This principal image is venerated for its reputed efficacy in gathering faith to treat women's illnesses, other ailments, and to promote marital harmony. == History == The temple was founded in 1638. Initially, the temple complex included the large main hall and various subsidiary halls, along with an adjacent park area to the north of the precincts. In 1698, it became the object of a petition by the 113th Emperor of Higashiyama. During the mid-Meiji period (1868–1912), the main hall and other structures were demolished, and the temple grounds were reduced to their current scale. == Address == [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]] [[:en:Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|Fushimi Ward]] Masuya-cho 614 == Principal image == In the main hall, the principal image of the Great Seimen Kongō is enshrined. This hidden buddha (秘仏) is housed within a portable shrine (厨子内). It is exhibited only once every 60 years, in the year of {{ill|Koushin|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koushin|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (kanoesaru). It is believed to ward off severe illnesses—excluding those severe, contagious conditions known as "denshiki" (伝死鬼). Since such diseases were thought to be transmitted by unseen insects, it was believed that defeating the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) would expel these maladies, thus becoming syncretized with Koushin. Since ancient times, ritual practices have been performed here to expel all malignant spirits and demons and to fulfill all prayers. The "Seimen Kongō Dharma" is recited daily at the temple, and an imperial regalia display (御前立) is also present. It is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|ja|真言宗醍醐派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|1=Shingon-shū Daigo-ha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10437874}}, enshrining its principal image, the {{ill|Great Seimen Kongō|ja|大青面金剛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Seimen Kongō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (dai-shōmen kongō, also known as "Koushin-san"). This principal image is venerated for its reputed efficacy in gathering faith to treat women's illnesses, other ailments, and to promote marital harmony. == History == The temple was founded in 1638. Initially, the temple complex included the large main hall and various subsidiary halls, along with an adjacent park area to the north of the precincts. In 1698, it became the object of a petition by the 113th Emperor of Higashiyama. During the mid-Meiji period (1868–1912), the main hall and other structures were demolished, and the temple grounds were reduced to their current scale. == Address == [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]] [[:en:Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|Fushimi Ward]] Masuya-cho 614 == Principal image == In the main hall, the principal image of the Great Seimen Kongō is enshrined. This hidden buddha (秘仏) is housed within a portable shrine (厨子内). It is exhibited only once every 60 years, in the year of {{ill|Koushin|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koushin|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (kanoesaru). It is believed to ward off severe illnesses—excluding those severe, contagious conditions known as "denshiki" (伝死鬼). Since such diseases were thought to be transmitted by unseen insects, it was believed that defeating the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) would expel these maladies, thus becoming syncretized with Koushin. Since ancient times, ritual practices have been performed here to expel all malignant spirits and demons and to fulfill all prayers. The "Seimen Kongō Dharma" is recited daily at the temple, and an imperial regalia display (御前立) is also present. Additionally, attendant deities enshrined include four Great Yasha, two Great Child Attendants, the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|Three Monkeys]] (see no evil, speak no evil, hear no evil), a waiting attendant (脇待), a statue of [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] from the Kamakura period, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|弘法大師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūkai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 空海|1=Kūkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q222227}}, and a tablet dedicated to [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]. == About Koushin == The term "Koushin" is derived from the combination of the [[:en:Heavenly Stems|Tenkan]] (the ten Heavenly Stems: kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki) and the [[:en:Earthly Branches|Jūnishi]] (the twelve Earthly Branches: ne, ushi, tora, u, tatsu, mi, uma, hi, saru, tori, inu, i). There are 60 possible combinations, which recur once every 60 days. On the night of a Koushin day, people remain awake in an event known as "Koushin-machi" (waiting for Koushin). It is believed that the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) reside therein. When people eventually fall asleep on a Koushin night, these insects are thought to crawl out of the body. The insects were believed to report the misdeeds committed by a person to the heavenly emperor, thereby determining that person's lifespan. Thus, by staying awake during Koushin-machi, people pray for protection, longevity, and the purity of body and spirit. == Amulets == === Kukkuri Saru === This amulet depicts a monkey with its arms and legs bound, rendering it immobile. The monkey symbolizes the desires that reside in the human heart. Just as a monkey may scurry about, these desires are thought to move within the heart, causing one to neglect effort, commit misdeeds, or hinder the fulfillment of one's wishes. By binding these desires, it is believed that Koushin controls them. Within the Kukkuri Saru, a talisman of the Great Seimen Kongō is housed, and through the secret technique of "kaigen" (awakening), a soul is imbued. == Annual Events == * Konnyaku Fūji Dai Kigan-kai (a rigorous konnyaku prayer ritual aimed at curing all diseases, joint pain, and lower back pain) (January 7) * Hoshi Matsuri (a ritual for good fortune and exorcism at the start of spring, featuring a great fire ritual with Shaitō Daigoma and fire-walking training) (February 11) * Spring Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Vernal Equinox) * Seirei Mukae Gane (a bell-ringing ritual during Obon) * Shōgaki Kuyō-kai (a Hungry Ghost Memorial Service) (August 16) * Autumn Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Autumnal Equinox) * Women's Guardian Prayer Meeting (a rigorous prayer meeting for women's happiness and protection) (3rd Sunday of October) * Emperor Higashiyama Memorial and Columbarium Mass (December 17) * Joya no Kane (bell ringing on New Year's Eve, December 31) * Monthly memorial services for ancestors and mizuko (water child) memorial services, as well as Koushin Goma (on the 7th day of every month) * Prayer meetings (on Koushin days) {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Kyoto Prefecture}} }} {{translated page|ja|明壽院|version=101308058|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| {{draft categories| [[Category:ill|Temples in Fushimi Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市伏見区の寺}} }} [[ja:明壽院]] [[Category:Temples in Fushimi-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:Temples of the Shingon Daigo branch]] {{wikidata link|Q85880838}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

{{ill|Yamamura Bishamondō|ja|山村毘沙門堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamura Bishamondō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 円満寺 (奈良市)|1=Enman-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394813}} | 札所等 = | 文化財 = | 公式HP = | 公式HP名 = | 地図 = Japan Kyoto city | 地図2 = }} '''Myoju-in''' (みょうじゅいん, Myoju-in) is a temple, also known as '''Fushimi Kōshin-dō''' (ふしみ こうしんどう), associated with [[:en:Kōshin|Koushin Faith]]. Its mountain name is '''Kōenzan''' (こうえんざん). It is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|ja|真言宗醍醐派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|1=Shingon-shū Daigo-ha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10437874}}, enshrining its principal image, the {{ill|Great Seimen Kongō|ja|大青面金剛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Seimen Kongō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (dai-shōmen kongō, also known as "Koushin-san"). This principal image is venerated for its reputed efficacy in gathering faith to treat women's illnesses, other ailments, and to promote marital harmony. == History == The temple was founded in 1638. Initially, the temple complex included the large main hall and various subsidiary halls, along with an adjacent park area to the north of the precincts. In 1698, it became the object of a petition by the 113th Emperor of Higashiyama. During the mid-Meiji period (1868–1912), the main hall and other structures were demolished, and the temple grounds were reduced to their current scale. == Address == [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]] [[:en:Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|Fushimi Ward]] Masuya-cho 614 == Principal image == In the main hall, the principal image of the Great Seimen Kongō is enshrined. This hidden buddha (秘仏) is housed within a portable shrine (厨子内). It is exhibited only once every 60 years, in the year of {{ill|Koushin|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koushin|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (kanoesaru). It is believed to ward off severe illnesses—excluding those severe, contagious conditions known as "denshiki" (伝死鬼). Since such diseases were thought to be transmitted by unseen insects, it was believed that defeating the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) would expel these maladies, thus becoming syncretized with Koushin. Since ancient times, ritual practices have been performed here to expel all malignant spirits and demons and to fulfill all prayers. The "Seimen Kongō Dharma" is recited daily at the temple, and an imperial regalia display (御前立) is also present. Additionally, attendant deities enshrined include four Great Yasha, two Great Child Attendants, the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|Three Monkeys]] (see no evil, speak no evil, hear no evil), a waiting attendant (脇待), a statue of [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] from the Kamakura period, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|弘法大師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūkai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 空海|1=Kūkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q222227}}, and a tablet dedicated to [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]. == About Koushin == The term "Koushin" is derived from the combination of the [[:en:Heavenly Stems|Tenkan]] (the ten Heavenly Stems: kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki) and the [[:en:Earthly Branches|Jūnishi]] (the twelve Earthly Branches: ne, ushi, tora, u, tatsu, mi, uma, hi, saru, tori, inu, i). There are 60 possible combinations, which recur once every 60 days. On the night of a Koushin day, people remain awake in an event known as "Koushin-machi" (waiting for Koushin). It is believed that the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) reside therein. When people eventually fall asleep on a Koushin night, these insects are thought to crawl out of the body. The insects were believed to report the misdeeds committed by a person to the heavenly emperor, thereby determining that person's lifespan. Thus, by staying awake during Koushin-machi, people pray for protection, longevity, and the purity of body and spirit. == Amulets == === Kukkuri Saru === This amulet depicts a monkey with its arms and legs bound, rendering it immobile. The monkey symbolizes the desires that reside in the human heart. Just as a monkey may scurry about, these desires are thought to move within the heart, causing one to neglect effort, commit misdeeds, or hinder the fulfillment of one's wishes. By binding these desires, it is believed that Koushin controls them. Within the Kukkuri Saru, a talisman of the Great Seimen Kongō is housed, and through the secret technique of "kaigen" (awakening), a soul is imbued. == Annual Events == * Konnyaku Fūji Dai Kigan-kai (a rigorous konnyaku prayer ritual aimed at curing all diseases, joint pain, and lower back pain) (January 7) * Hoshi Matsuri (a ritual for good fortune and exorcism at the start of spring, featuring a great fire ritual with Shaitō Daigoma and fire-walking training) (February 11) * Spring Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Vernal Equinox) * Seirei Mukae Gane (a bell-ringing ritual during Obon) * Shōgaki Kuyō-kai (a Hungry Ghost Memorial Service) (August 16) * Autumn Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Autumnal Equinox) * Women's Guardian Prayer Meeting (a rigorous prayer meeting for women's happiness and protection) (3rd Sunday of October) * Emperor Higashiyama Memorial and Columbarium Mass (December 17) * Joya no Kane (bell ringing on New Year's Eve, December 31) * Monthly memorial services for ancestors and mizuko (water child) memorial services, as well as Koushin Goma (on the 7th day of every month) * Prayer meetings (on Koushin days) {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Kyoto Prefecture}} }} {{translated page|ja|明壽院|version=101308058|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| {{draft categories| [[Category:ill|Temples in Fushimi Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市伏見区の寺}} }} [[ja:明壽院]] [[Category:Temples in Fushimi-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:Temples of the Shingon Daigo branch]] {{wikidata link|Q85880838}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

When people eventually fall asleep on a Koushin night, these insects are thought to crawl out of the body. The insects were believed to report the misdeeds committed by a person to the heavenly emperor, thereby determining that person's lifespan. Thus, by staying awake during Koushin-machi, people pray for protection, longevity, and the purity of body and spirit. == Amulets == === Kukkuri Saru === This amulet depicts a monkey with its arms and legs bound, rendering it immobile. The monkey symbolizes the desires that reside in the human heart. Just as a monkey may scurry about, these desires are thought to move within the heart, causing one to neglect effort, commit misdeeds, or hinder the fulfillment of one's wishes. By binding these desires, it is believed that Koushin controls them. Within the Kukkuri Saru, a talisman of the Great Seimen Kongō is housed, and through the secret technique of "kaigen" (awakening), a soul is imbued. == Annual Events == * Konnyaku Fūji Dai Kigan-kai (a rigorous konnyaku prayer ritual aimed at curing all diseases, joint pain, and lower back pain) (January 7) * Hoshi Matsuri (a ritual for good fortune and exorcism at the start of spring, featuring a great fire ritual with Shaitō Daigoma and fire-walking training) (February 11) * Spring Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Vernal Equinox) * Seirei Mukae Gane (a bell-ringing ritual during Obon) * Shōgaki Kuyō-kai (a Hungry Ghost Memorial Service) (August 16) * Autumn Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Autumnal Equinox) * Women's Guardian Prayer Meeting (a rigorous prayer meeting for women's happiness and protection) (3rd Sunday of October) * Emperor Higashiyama Memorial and Columbarium Mass (December 17) * Joya no Kane (bell ringing on New Year's Eve, December 31) * Monthly memorial services for ancestors and mizuko (water child) memorial services, as well as Koushin Goma (on the 7th day of every month) * Prayer meetings (on Koushin days) {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Kyoto Prefecture}} }} {{translated page|ja|明壽院|version=101308058|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| {{draft categories| [[Category:ill|Temples in Fushimi Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市伏見区の寺}} }} [[ja:明壽院]] [[Category:Temples in Fushimi-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:Temples of the Shingon Daigo branch]] {{wikidata link|Q85880838}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[ja:明壽院]] [[ja:明壽院]] [[ja:明壽院]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Myoju-in, Fushimi.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon-shū Daigo-ha|WD=Q10437874}}|prefecture={{ill|Fushimi-ku|WD=Q1197771}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.94252777777778|N|135.76047222222223|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q85880838]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Referencesの明記|date=November 2019}} {{infobox religious building | name = {{ill|Myoju-in|ja|明壽院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myoju-in|qq=}} | native_name = Myoju-in | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] | image = [[File:Myoju-in, Fushimi.jpg|300px]] | image_description = | address = [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]] [[:en:Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|Fushimi Ward]] Masuya-cho 614 | 位置 = | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 56 | 緯度秒 = 33.1 | 経度度 = 135 | 経度分 = 45 | 経度秒 = 37.7 | 山号 = {{ill|Kōenzan|ja|光圓山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōenzan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | 院号 = | 宗旨 = | 宗派 = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|ja|真言宗醍醐派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|1=Shingon-shū Daigo-ha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10437874}} | 寺格 = | 本尊 = {{ill|Seimen Kongō|ja|青面金剛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seimen Kongō|1=Shōmen Kongō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11662826}} | 創建年 = [[:en:1638|1638]] | 開山 = | 開基 = | 中興年 = | 中興 = | 正式名 = | 別称 = {{ill|Fushimi Kōshin-dō|ja|伏見Kōshin-dō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fushimi Kōshin-dō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{ill|Yamamura Bishamondō|ja|山村毘沙門堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamura Bishamondō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 円満寺 (奈良市)|1=Enman-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394813}} | 札所等 = | 文化財 = | 公式HP = | 公式HP名 = | 地図 = Japan Kyoto city | 地図2 = }} '''Myoju-in''' (みょうじゅいん, Myoju-in) is a temple, also known as '''Fushimi Kōshin-dō''' (ふしみ こうしんどう), associated with [[:en:Kōshin|Koushin Faith]]. Its mountain name is '''Kōenzan''' (こうえんざん). It is a temple of the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|ja|真言宗醍醐派|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shingon Buddhism Daigo-ha|1=Shingon-shū Daigo-ha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10437874}}, enshrining its principal image, the {{ill|Great Seimen Kongō|ja|大青面金剛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Seimen Kongō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (dai-shōmen kongō, also known as "Koushin-san"). This principal image is venerated for its reputed efficacy in gathering faith to treat women's illnesses, other ailments, and to promote marital harmony. == History == The temple was founded in 1638. Initially, the temple complex included the large main hall and various subsidiary halls, along with an adjacent park area to the north of the precincts. In 1698, it became the object of a petition by the 113th Emperor of Higashiyama. During the mid-Meiji period (1868–1912), the main hall and other structures were demolished, and the temple grounds were reduced to their current scale. == Address == [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto City]] [[:en:Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|Fushimi Ward]] Masuya-cho 614 == Principal image == In the main hall, the principal image of the Great Seimen Kongō is enshrined. This hidden buddha (秘仏) is housed within a portable shrine (厨子内). It is exhibited only once every 60 years, in the year of {{ill|Koushin|ja|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koushin|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (kanoesaru). It is believed to ward off severe illnesses—excluding those severe, contagious conditions known as "denshiki" (伝死鬼). Since such diseases were thought to be transmitted by unseen insects, it was believed that defeating the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) would expel these maladies, thus becoming syncretized with Koushin. Since ancient times, ritual practices have been performed here to expel all malignant spirits and demons and to fulfill all prayers. The "Seimen Kongō Dharma" is recited daily at the temple, and an imperial regalia display (御前立) is also present. Additionally, attendant deities enshrined include four Great Yasha, two Great Child Attendants, the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|Three Monkeys]] (see no evil, speak no evil, hear no evil), a waiting attendant (脇待), a statue of [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] from the Kamakura period, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|弘法大師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūkai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 空海|1=Kūkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q222227}}, and a tablet dedicated to [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]. == About Koushin == The term "Koushin" is derived from the combination of the [[:en:Heavenly Stems|Tenkan]] (the ten Heavenly Stems: kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki) and the [[:en:Earthly Branches|Jūnishi]] (the twelve Earthly Branches: ne, ushi, tora, u, tatsu, mi, uma, hi, saru, tori, inu, i). There are 60 possible combinations, which recur once every 60 days. On the night of a Koushin day, people remain awake in an event known as "Koushin-machi" (waiting for Koushin). It is believed that the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) reside therein. When people eventually fall asleep on a Koushin night, these insects are thought to crawl out of the body. The insects were believed to report the misdeeds committed by a person to the heavenly emperor, thereby determining that person's lifespan. Thus, by staying awake during Koushin-machi, people pray for protection, longevity, and the purity of body and spirit. == Amulets == === Kukkuri Saru === This amulet depicts a monkey with its arms and legs bound, rendering it immobile. The monkey symbolizes the desires that reside in the human heart. Just as a monkey may scurry about, these desires are thought to move within the heart, causing one to neglect effort, commit misdeeds, or hinder the fulfillment of one's wishes. By binding these desires, it is believed that Koushin controls them. Within the Kukkuri Saru, a talisman of the Great Seimen Kongō is housed, and through the secret technique of "kaigen" (awakening), a soul is imbued. == Annual Events == * Konnyaku Fūji Dai Kigan-kai (a rigorous konnyaku prayer ritual aimed at curing all diseases, joint pain, and lower back pain) (January 7) * Hoshi Matsuri (a ritual for good fortune and exorcism at the start of spring, featuring a great fire ritual with Shaitō Daigoma and fire-walking training) (February 11) * Spring Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Vernal Equinox) * Seirei Mukae Gane (a bell-ringing ritual during Obon) * Shōgaki Kuyō-kai (a Hungry Ghost Memorial Service) (August 16) * Autumn Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Autumnal Equinox) * Women's Guardian Prayer Meeting (a rigorous prayer meeting for women's happiness and protection) (3rd Sunday of October) * Emperor Higashiyama Memorial and Columbarium Mass (December 17) * Joya no Kane (bell ringing on New Year's Eve, December 31) * Monthly memorial services for ancestors and mizuko (water child) memorial services, as well as Koushin Goma (on the 7th day of every month) * Prayer meetings (on Koushin days) {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Kyoto Prefecture}} }} {{translated page|ja|明壽院|version=101308058|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| {{draft categories| [[Category:ill|Temples in Fushimi Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市伏見区の寺}} }} [[ja:明壽院]] [[Category:Temples in Fushimi-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:Temples of the Shingon Daigo branch]] {{wikidata link|Q85880838}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

The "Seimen Kongō Dharma" is recited daily at the temple, and an imperial regalia display (御前立) is also present. Additionally, attendant deities enshrined include four Great Yasha, two Great Child Attendants, the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|Three Monkeys]] (see no evil, speak no evil, hear no evil), a waiting attendant (脇待), a statue of [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] from the Kamakura period, {{ill|Kūkai|ja|弘法大師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kūkai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 空海|1=Kūkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q222227}}, and a tablet dedicated to [[:en:Emperor Higashiyama|Emperor Higashiyama]]. == About Koushin == The term "Koushin" is derived from the combination of the [[:en:Heavenly Stems|Tenkan]] (the ten Heavenly Stems: kō, otsu, hei, tei, bo, ki, kō, shin, jin, ki) and the [[:en:Earthly Branches|Jūnishi]] (the twelve Earthly Branches: ne, ushi, tora, u, tatsu, mi, uma, hi, saru, tori, inu, i). There are 60 possible combinations, which recur once every 60 days. On the night of a Koushin day, people remain awake in an event known as "Koushin-machi" (waiting for Koushin). It is believed that the "Sanshi no Mushi" (three insects that dwell within the human body) reside therein. When people eventually fall asleep on a Koushin night, these insects are thought to crawl out of the body. The insects were believed to report the misdeeds committed by a person to the heavenly emperor, thereby determining that person's lifespan. Thus, by staying awake during Koushin-machi, people pray for protection, longevity, and the purity of body and spirit. == Amulets == === Kukkuri Saru === This amulet depicts a monkey with its arms and legs bound, rendering it immobile. The monkey symbolizes the desires that reside in the human heart. Just as a monkey may scurry about, these desires are thought to move within the heart, causing one to neglect effort, commit misdeeds, or hinder the fulfillment of one's wishes. By binding these desires, it is believed that Koushin controls them. Within the Kukkuri Saru, a talisman of the Great Seimen Kongō is housed, and through the secret technique of "kaigen" (awakening), a soul is imbued. == Annual Events == * Konnyaku Fūji Dai Kigan-kai (a rigorous konnyaku prayer ritual aimed at curing all diseases, joint pain, and lower back pain) (January 7) * Hoshi Matsuri (a ritual for good fortune and exorcism at the start of spring, featuring a great fire ritual with Shaitō Daigoma and fire-walking training) (February 11) * Spring Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Vernal Equinox) * Seirei Mukae Gane (a bell-ringing ritual during Obon) * Shōgaki Kuyō-kai (a Hungry Ghost Memorial Service) (August 16) * Autumn Equinox Midday Memorial Service (on the Autumnal Equinox) * Women's Guardian Prayer Meeting (a rigorous prayer meeting for women's happiness and protection) (3rd Sunday of October) * Emperor Higashiyama Memorial and Columbarium Mass (December 17) * Joya no Kane (bell ringing on New Year's Eve, December 31) * Monthly memorial services for ancestors and mizuko (water child) memorial services, as well as Koushin Goma (on the 7th day of every month) * Prayer meetings (on Koushin days) {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Kyoto Prefecture}} }} {{translated page|ja|明壽院|version=101308058|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| {{draft categories| {{wikidata link|Q85880838}} [[Category:ill|Temples in Fushimi Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市伏見区の寺}} }} [[ja:明壽院]] [[Category:Kōshin]] [[Category:Temples in Fushimi-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Temples of the Shingon Daigo branch]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
